examples.title: Halting Problem Decider (Impossible)
examples.description: Demonstrates the structure assumed by incorrect proofs - why it cannot exist
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows why the Halting Problem is undecidable. Any assumed decider H leads to a paradox when we construct a machine D that does the opposite of what H predicts for its own description.

examples.title: Deciding Recursive Language
examples.description: How to decide a recursive (decidable) language - TM that always halts
examples.explanation: A recursive language has a TM that always halts. For any input, it either accepts or rejects - no looping. This is the definition of decidability.

examples.title: Semi-Deciding RE Language
examples.description: TM that accepts strings in RE language but may loop on rejection
examples.explanation: An RE language has a TM that accepts all strings in L but may not halt on strings outside L. It may loop forever. The Acceptance Problem A_TM is the classic example of RE but not recursive.

examples.title: Deciding DFA Emptiness (Decidable)
examples.description: Algorithm to check if language of DFA is empty - always halts
examples.explanation: Unlike TM emptiness which is undecidable, DFA emptiness is easily decidable. We just need to check if any accepting state is reachable from the start state. This is a fundamental result: emptiness for regular languages is decidable.

examples.title: Proof by Reduction - A_TM to E_TM
examples.description: Showing how to reduce acceptance problem to emptiness problem
examples.explanation: This shows how to prove undecidability by reduction. If we could decide emptiness (E_TM), we could decide acceptance (A_TM). Since A_TM is known undecidable, E_TM must also be undecidable. This is the standard technique for proving problems undecidable.
